main_summary.summary: Dr. Robert B. Hayling (b. November 20, 1929, Tallahassee, Florida), the St. Augustine dentist who led the local Civil Rights movement in 1963-64, recounts his path from Florida A&M and Air Force service through Meharry Medical College and into the leadership of the St. Augustine campaign that helped force passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine on September 14, 2011 for the Civil Rights History Project. Hayling describes Air Force toxicology work at Wright-Patterson, dental training at Meharry under Z. Alexander Looby, the April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall after the Looby bombing, his 1960 arrival in St. Augustine to inherit Dr. Rudolph Gordon's practice, and his recruitment as advisor to the NAACP Youth Council after Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr. was forced to flee to Gainesville. Central episodes include the Quadricentennial exclusion of Black St. Augustinians, George Reedy's double-cross of Hayling's delegation before the Lyndon B. Johnson visit, the September 1963 Klan rally where Hayling and three others were beaten with ax handles and threatened with immolation, the break with Roy Wilkins and the NAACP, Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr.'s Yale spring-break tutorial volunteers, Mary Peabody's arrest at the Monson Motor Lodge, and Hayling's refusal to honor the LBJ-requested demonstration pause. Closes with his 1966 relocation to Cocoa, Florida and the June 20, 2003 naming of Dr. Hayling Place.
key_themes: SCLC and Yale Divinity School alliance
Civil Rights Act of 1964 passage pressure
main_summary.historical_significance:
Hayling is the principal eyewitness to the St. Augustine movement, the campaign whose escalation in spring and early summer 1964 created the political pressure that helped force Lyndon B. Johnson and the Senate to complete passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 on July 2, 1964. The interview supplies first-person testimony on three episodes that bear directly on the historical record: the federal-government double-cross orchestrated by George Reedy in advance of the Vice President's 1963 Quadricentennial visit; the September 1963 Klan rally at which Hayling, James Hauser, James Jackson, and Clyde Jenkins were beaten and threatened with being doused in gasoline and set on fire before the Klan's announced intent was interrupted by an Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach who recorded the rally and called the Florida Highway Patrol; and Hayling's explicit refusal, when LBJ asked civil rights organizations for a two-week cessation of demonstrations, to comply on behalf of the St. Augustine chapter ("fool me once, shame on you; fool me twice, shame on me").

Hayling also documents the institutional rupture between the local St. Augustine movement and Roy Wilkins's national NAACP, in which the local chapter surrendered its charter rather than accept instructions from New York, and the subsequent alliance with Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s SCLC and Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr.'s Yale Divinity School. The testimony names the network of Northern white financial supporters (Esther Burgess, Mrs. Rowe, Mrs. Campbell, Mary Peabody) and the local Black support circle (Henry Twine, Oscar Turner, Clyde Jenkins, Fannie Fulwood, Elizabeth Hawthorne, E.G. Chase, Mr. Pennington) who sustained the campaign when both the federal government and the national NAACP declined to back it.

chapters.title: Tallahassee Roots and Family
chapters.summary: Hayling describes his November 20, 1929 birth in Tallahassee to a Florida A&M tailoring instructor (father from Grenada) and a Creole homemaker mother from New Orleans. The four Hayling siblings, Charles Hayling Jr., Francis Hayling, Yvonne Hayling Clark, and Robert, all attended Florida A&M and earned advanced degrees.

chapters.title: Air Force Service and Toxicology
chapters.summary: Hayling describes entering the Air Force in August 1951, basic training at Lackland Air Force Base, medical-technician training at Sheppard Air Force Base, and graduation from Officer Candidate School in December 1952. He was assigned to Wright-Patterson Air Force Base in Dayton, Ohio as an aeromedical researcher in toxicology, where he declined an early promotion to captain in order to enter dental school.

chapters.title: Meharry and the Looby Bombing
chapters.summary: Hayling enrolls at Meharry Medical College in Nashville in September 1955 after marrying his Florida A&M sweetheart. His dental jurisprudence instructor is Z. Alexander Looby, lead attorney for Nashville school desegregation. After Looby's home is dynamited in April 1960, blowing out windows of Hayling's adjacent Meharry dormitory, Hayling joins the April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall and meets John Lewis, Diane Nash, C.T. Vivian, James Bevel, and other student-movement leaders.
related_events: April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall

chapters.title: Florida Movements and Coming to St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Hayling recounts the Tallahassee movements led by Rev. C.K. Steele (bus desegregation) and Patricia Stephens Due and Priscilla Stephens (movie-theater and restaurant desegregation), including the sisters' imprisonment in a wire-fenced Tallahassee jail with no privacy. Florida's tuition-supplement program for Black dentists at Meharry, combined with the GI Bill, funded his training. He worked summers as a sky-cap at Newark Airport and a Sheepshead Bay seafood waiter, then chose St. Augustine in July 1960 over Panama City because the late Dr. Rudolph Gordon's practice was available there.

chapters.title: Setting Up Practice in St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Hayling inherits Dr. Gordon's office and 50-60% white patient roster in late 1960. He becomes the city and county jail dentist and the state-prison-farm dentist for St. Johns County, in part because white dentists were uncomfortable with armed deputies accompanying prisoner-patients. Sheriff L.O. Davis becomes a private patient. Hayling describes the self-financed payment plans that sustained a low-cash-economy Black practice and the early absence of any racial-rights conflict.

chapters.title: Youth Council Advisor and the Quadricentennial
chapters.summary: After Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr. is forced out of St. Augustine and relocates to Gainesville (Mount Carmel Baptist Church), a delegation of NAACP Youth Council members asks Hayling to take over as advisor in 1963. Local NAACP president Fannie Fulwood and secretary Elizabeth Hawthorne become his stabilizers. The federal government appropriates $400,000 for St. Augustine's 400th-anniversary Quadricentennial celebrations; no Black St. Augustinians are appointed to any committee, despite Black residents comprising roughly 20% of the city. The exclusion becomes the wake-up moment that crystallizes the local movement.

chapters.title: The George Reedy Double-Cross
chapters.summary: Hayling and his delegation write to Vice President Lyndon B. Johnson protesting the segregated Quadricentennial dinner at the Ponce de Leon Hotel. LBJ aide George Reedy visits the office repeatedly during the week before the dinner and persuades the delegation to suspend demonstrations in exchange for a meeting with the City Commission. Six or eight delegates are seated in an alcove above the Grand Ballroom, separated from the dinner. The next morning's meeting brings only the Mayor's Secretary with a tape recorder, no commissioners, no Mayor, no federal representative. The city simultaneously dresses a Black Lincolnville-community night patrolman in a uniform and parks him at the Old Slave Market to refute Hayling's claim that the force had no Black officers.
related_events: Vice President LBJ St. Augustine visit 1963

chapters.title: The September 1963 Klan Rally Beating
chapters.summary: Hayling, James Jackson, James Hauser, and Clyde Jenkins drive to observe a major Klan rally outside St. Augustine. Connie Lynch of California and J.B. Stoner of Atlanta are scheduled speakers. The four are surrounded by armed Klansmen at a dug-out moat across the road, dragged to the rally stage, piled like cordwood, and beaten with ax handles and baseball bats. The rally announcer prepares the crowd to smell "a nigger burn" with gasoline. An Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach who infiltrated the rally with a personal recorder calls the Florida Highway Patrol; Sheriff L.O. Davis sends auxiliaries. The four are evacuated by E.G. Chase's hearse to Brewster Hospital in Jacksonville. Hayling loses 11 teeth and sustains right-arm damage that nearly ends his dental career. Hoss Manucy, the local Klan leader, was a convicted moonshiner deputized along with Klan members as auxiliary deputies under Sheriff Davis.
related_events: September 1963 St. Augustine Klan rally beating

chapters.title: Break with NAACP, Alliance with SCLC and Yale
chapters.summary: Roy Wilkins phones from NAACP New York demanding that Hayling follow national instructions or surrender the St. Augustine charter. Hayling sends the charter back by special delivery. He travels to Atlanta on weekends and meets Julian Bond, SNCC organizers, Ella Baker, and members of Ebenezer Baptist Church. Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr. of Yale Divinity School organizes a spring-break tutorial deployment in which Yale students live with Black St. Augustine families, conduct cleanup and tutoring, and return home to mount a stage drama portraying L.O. Davis and Judge Charles Mathis. Northern white supporters, Esther Burgess (wife of Bishop John Melville Burgess), Mrs. Rowe, Mrs. Campbell, and Mary Peabody (mother of Massachusetts Governor Endicott "Chub" Peabody), provide financial backing and bodies on the ground.
related_events: Yale spring-break St. Augustine tutorial program

chapters.title: Refusing the LBJ Pause and Civil Rights Act 1964
chapters.summary: When President Lyndon B. Johnson asks civil rights organizations for a two-week cessation of demonstrations to ease passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, Dr. King and the national organizations agree. Hayling refuses on behalf of St. Augustine, "fool me once, shame on you, fool me twice, shame on me", invoking the Reedy double-cross. Mary Peabody is arrested with Hayling at the Monson Motor Lodge. James Brock, owner of the Monson, becomes infamous for the photograph of him pouring acid into the motel pool while integrated swimmers are in the water. Dr. King is in St. Augustine on June 30, 1964; the Civil Rights Act of 1964 is signed shortly after.

chapters.title: Cocoa Relocation and ACCORD Recognition
chapters.summary: After fulfilling the five-year Florida tuition obligation, Hayling relocates to Cocoa, Florida with help from mortician Rudy Stone, opening a practice next to Dr. Jerkins that serves NASA Cape Kennedy workers until LBJ moves the control center to the Johnson Space Center in Houston. Hayling returns to St. Augustine in retirement; on June 20, 2003 the city renames Scott Street as Dr. Hayling Place, championed by Commissioner Earl Jones (son of Hayling's longtime office assistant Maddie Jones). The city gives him the Award of Valor on July 2, 2011 at the ACCORD ceremony, where Dorothy Cotton speaks and the Rev. Goldie Eubanks family is honored. Hayling's daughter Robin (with husband David, from Mississippi), his younger daughter (a Yale graduate with a Stanford MBA) and her husband Chris (a Princeton and Harvard Business School graduate working for Apple Inc. in the Far East), and his grandsons Roman and Avery attend the event.
related_events: June 20, 2003 Dr. Hayling Place street naming

loc_video.caption: Robert Bagner Hayling oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Saint Augustine, Florida, 2011 September 14
